title: Por vs Para
description: Two little words that both translate as English 'for' — but they are not interchangeable. Learn PARA for destination, recipient, deadline and purpose, and POR for cause, movement, duration, exchange and means, plus the most common fixed expressions.

cards.notes: **PARA** points to a **destination** or end-goal — where something is headed. *Salgo para Madrid* = I'm leaving for (heading to) Madrid. Think of *para* as an arrow → pointing forward to a target.
**PARA** marks the **recipient** — who receives something. *Este regalo es para ti* = This gift is for you. Careful: *por ti* would mean 'because of you' or 'on your behalf', not the recipient!
**PARA** + a time = a **deadline**, the point by which something must be done. *La tarea es para el lunes* = The homework is for/by Monday. Do not confuse with *por*, which expresses duration.
**PARA + infinitive** = purpose, 'in order to'. *Estudio para aprender* = I study (in order) to learn. Whenever English means 'in order to', use *para*, never *por*.

cards.front: Para mí, es difícil. / Para un niño, lee bien.
cards.back: For me / In my opinion... ; For a child, he reads well. (opinion, comparison)
cards.notes: **PARA** introduces an **opinion** (*Para mí...* = In my opinion / For me) and a **comparison/contrast** (*Para un niño, lee muy bien* = For a child, he reads very well — i.e. compared with what you'd expect).

cards.front: Lo hice por amor. / Gracias por todo.
cards.back: I did it for/out of love. ; Thanks for everything. (cause / reason)
cards.notes: **POR** expresses **cause or reason** — the motive *behind* an action (looking backward). *Por amor* = out of love; *Gracias por todo* = thanks for everything. English 'for' here means 'because of'.
**POR** = movement **through, along or around** a place. *Caminamos por el parque* = We walk through the park. Here English uses 'through/along', but learners often reach for the wrong word.
**POR** + a span of time = **duration**, how long something lasts. *Por dos horas* = for two hours. This is a classic trap: English 'for two hours' is **por**, NOT para.

cards.back: I bought it for 10 euros. (exchange / price)
cards.notes: **POR** marks **exchange or price** — one thing traded for another. *Lo compré por 10 euros* = I bought it for 10 euros. *Gracias por el regalo* and *Te cambio mi libro por el tuyo* also use this exchange sense.

cards.front: por teléfono / dos veces por semana / escrito por
cards.back: by phone ; twice per week ; written by (means, 'per', agent)
cards.notes: **POR** covers three more uses: **means** (*por teléfono, por avión* = by phone, by plane), **'per'** (*dos veces por semana* = twice per week), and the **agent** in passive sentences (*escrito por Cervantes* = written by Cervantes). Also substitution: *Trabajo por ti* = I work in your place.

cards.front: por favor, por fin, por eso, por supuesto, por ejemplo
cards.back: please ; finally/at last ; that's why ; of course ; for example
cards.notes: High-frequency **fixed expressions with POR**: *por favor* (please), *por fin* (finally), *por eso* (that's why / for that reason), *por supuesto* (of course), *por ejemplo* (for example). Learn them as whole chunks.
A common **fixed expression with PARA**: *para siempre* = forever. *Te querré para siempre* = I'll love you forever. Note it is *para*, fitting the goal/endpoint logic of *para*.

steps.title: Two words, one English 'for'
steps.body:
# Por vs Para

English has one little word — **for** — where Spanish has **two**: **por** and **para**. They are not interchangeable, and choosing the wrong one changes the meaning.

A quick mental model:

- **PARA** looks **forward** → it points to a **goal, destination, purpose, recipient or deadline**. Picture an arrow aiming at a target.
- **POR** looks at the **cause, the route, the exchange** behind or around an action. Picture the motive, the path travelled, or a swap.

Compare:

| Spanish | English | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Es **para** ti. | It's for you. | you are the **recipient** (goal) |
| Lo hago **por** ti. | I do it for you. | you are the **reason** / on your behalf |

> Same English 'for', completely different Spanish. The rest of this lesson sorts out exactly when to use each.

steps.title: Use-by-use reference table
steps.body:
## When to use each

**PARA — goal, forward-looking:**

| Use | Example | English |
|---|---|---|
| destination / goal | Salgo **para** Madrid. | I'm leaving for Madrid. |
| recipient | Es **para** ti. | It's for you. |
| deadline | Es **para** el lunes. | It's for/by Monday. |
| purpose ('in order to') | Estudio **para** aprender. | I study to learn. |
| opinion | **Para** mí, es fácil. | For me / In my opinion, it's easy. |
| comparison / contrast | **Para** un niño, lee bien. | For a child, he reads well. |

**POR — cause, route, exchange:**

| Use | Example | English |
|---|---|---|
| cause / reason | Gracias **por** todo. | Thanks for everything. |
| through / along | Caminamos **por** el parque. | We walk through the park. |
| duration | Estudié **por** dos horas. | I studied for two hours. |
| exchange / price | Lo compré **por** 10 euros. | I bought it for 10 euros. |
| means | **por** teléfono, **por** avión | by phone, by plane |
| 'per' | dos veces **por** semana | twice per week |
| agent (passive) | escrito **por** Cervantes | written by Cervantes |
| substitution | Trabajo **por** ti. | I work in your place. |

steps.title: Traps for English speakers
steps.body:
## The classic confusions

Because **both** translate as 'for', English speakers guess. Here are the traps to memorise:

**1. Purpose (para) vs cause (por).**
- *Estudio **para** aprender* = I study **in order to** learn (goal). 
- *Lo hago **por** amor* = I do it **because of / out of** love (reason).
- Quick test: if you can say 'in order to', use **para**; if you mean 'because of', use **por**.

**2. 'For two hours' = POR.**
- *Estudié **por** dos horas* — duration is always **por**, even though English says 'for'. (Many natives also drop the preposition entirely: *Estudié dos horas*.)

**3. 'For you' — it depends!**
- *Es **para** ti* = It's for you (you **receive** it).
- *Gracias **por** ti* / *Lo hago **por** ti* = thanks to you / I do it because of (or instead of) you.

**4. Fixed expressions — just memorise:**
- With **por**: *por favor, por fin, por eso, por supuesto, por ejemplo*.
- With **para**: *para siempre* (forever).

> When in doubt, ask: am I pointing at a **goal/target** (para) or at a **cause/route/exchange** (por)?

exercise.prompt: Match each use with the correct word, *por* or *para*.
Fill the blank: 'I study Spanish for (in order to help) my work.' Goal or cause?
exercise.hint: It expresses the goal/purpose ('in order to'). That's para.

exercise.prompt: Fill the blank: 'We talked for two hours.' Watch out — English 'for' here is duration!
exercise.hint: Duration (how long) is always por, never para.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *This gift is for you.* (you are the recipient)

exercise.hint: Thanks expresses the reason behind gratitude = por.

steps.title: Build: I bought it for 10 euros

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I bought it for 10 euros.* (exchange/price)
Fill the blank: 'The children run through the park.' (movement)
Which sentence means 'For me (in my opinion), Spanish is easy'?

images.label: Para mí, el español es fácil.
Por mí, el español es fácil.
Para yo, el español es fácil.
Por mi, el español es fácil.

exercise.hint: Opinion is introduced by para: 'Para mí...' (with accented mí).
distractors: Por mí, el español es fácil.
Para yo, el español es fácil.
Por mi, el español es fácil.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I talk with my mother by phone.* (means)
accept: Hablo con mi madre por teléfono.
hablo con mi madre por telefono
Hablo con mi madre por teléfono
Yo hablo con mi madre por teléfono.
exercise.hint: Means of communication = por: por teléfono.
distractors: Hablo con mi madre para teléfono.
Hablo con mi madre por telefóno.
Hablo con mi madre para teléfono

exercise.prompt: Fill the blank: 'The homework is for/by Monday.' (deadline)
exercise.hint: A deadline (the point by which) = para.
'forever' is the fixed expression para siempre.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish fixed expression with its English meaning.
